author: @DreamNotOfToday
text: There's a categorical difference between coddling or "participation trophies" (which is what creates "snowflakes") and instilling proper values, including safety considerations. Not everyone survives their "war wounds". Kid in my shop class, decades ago, got killed by a metal lathe, do you think my teacher went on a rant about how that's not his problem and people need to get hurt to learn? No, he took responsibility and seriously examined how he taught safety and restructured the class so it wouldn't happen again. Nobody's asking you to tell people to stop riding a metaphorical bicycle, they're asking you to let people know that when they ride that bike it's best to wear a helmet. Not everyone has a parent or someone there in their life who can watch them and tell them the minimum they need to stop themselves from getting killed - you don't know who watches your videos or what their life is like and frankly whether you wish it or not, you do have an a responsibility to do the bare minimum. When people talk about "snowflakes" it means people who can't take criticism or who think that they're special and the rules don't apply to them - which is exactly what you're doing here. Going on a rant about how you don't feel like you should take any responsibility for the actions you might inspire or how they might hurt themselves? Snowflake behavior. You're not special and the rules still apply to you. Going on a rant because some people rightly called you out for this flippant attitude? That's snowflake behavior. Listen to valid criticism and do some self reflection and take some responsibility.

author: @marquisdemoo1792
text:
As a serving soldier I used to help the Army Cadet Force (ACF) out on Annual Camp with Signal Training.  Many regular soldiers look down on the ACF, but as an organisation staffed by amateurs it does a wonderful job, albeit unsurprisingly not everything goes to plan.  In view of this I found that Signal training exercises in the field usually lost half the morning actually getting the cadets to the exercise area, so I hit on the idea of getting them out the night before and camping there.  That way if they were late arriving it didn't matter because, come the morning when the exercise started, they were in the right place and under my control.  

I did this on several occasions with Yorkshire ACF and a bunch of 14 - 17 year olds, which were split into 2 competing teams of 8 individuals in 2 separate camp sites, whilst the adults camped with the 4 tonner at another.  Of course in the evening and morning we visited them, but otherwise they were left to their own devices, which included cooking their meals and breaking camp to start the exercise in the morning.  On both occasions I remember being thanked profusely by these kids for trusting them enough to let them camp out without adults present, which they said nobody else had.  Of course if something had gone wrong I would have been in deep water, which I guess is why other adults didn't dare give these kids free reign.  In my book milksop kids become milksop adults.

author: @Rick_Bagnall
text: Thank You! This is what REAL life is about! I have a 12 year old son of a friend and a 9 year old granddaughter that i regularly have help me buildd different things, repair power equipment, and restore old tools. In my teaching them I use the same philosophy that i use to teach adults CNC machining: assess their levels of interest and skill (age appropriate of course), bring them along as i do things and explain things, and as they grasp the the concepts and skills required, transition them into doing it themselves while I make sure they are safe. I know they are ready to take the risk by themselves when they go through a process and sometimes ask me a question about it, even though they already know the answer but lack confidence, I "play dumb" at that point and that forces them to think through the process checking each step in their mind again. Of course I won't let the process proceed if they will be in danger of hurting seriously themselves or me. The greatest thrill I have is when they have run through it again in their mind  and press that button (or whatever) a little hesitantly and it works! My thrill is the look on their faces when they see the result that THEY have achieved and the confidence that instills to move forward and try other things.

author: @williamarmstrong7199
text:
100% right that is why chemistry is now more or less only taught theoretically. 
However when doing a Chemical Technicians Certificate at Wolverhampton Polly (that ages me) 
We each had Hydrogen Fluoride in squeety plastic bottles.    
Along side Conc Nitric, Piric (explosive when dry and always crusty dry deposits around the top)
Sulphuric etc etc. 
Did we fool around?? Well yes of course we did. But other than superficial burns from handling dry ice to take down the pub (wrapping it in paper towels is not good enough when in your trousers pocket.. i will say no more.) 
We survived. 
However the technicians who cleaned up after our experiments were not so lucky. 
All of them were missing ends of fingers or even two or three fingers from the Hydroflouric acid.
Hydrogen Fluoride soaks though most things including skin flesh and bone..  and then dissolves calcium bones... not nice and extremely painful.
So not all Health and safety is bunkum. Just 95% ;)
My early experiments with making things go BANG, today would have me in jail for making bombs. However only nearly blinded myself once.. that was a close call however having a face and eyes full of burning rocket fuel.. and only a muddy puddle close to to put out the flames... did not stop me for more than the time it took me to get my eyesight back again. Not to mention eye lashes, eyebrows and much of the hair on my head.  Should have joined the army as a sapper. ;)

Also as I was once told there are old electricians, there are bold electricians but there are no old bold electricians, some mistakes just don't provide a learning experience.

However is it your responsibility to highlight all the dangers involved in constructing your projects? Your projects don't usually seem to be instructions on how to use the tools, the tools are just a means to the end result.
If you are going to show how to use a piece of equipment then perhaps cover the basics of how not to permanently get maimed, but otherwise there are hundred of videos showing how to use most workshop equipment safely.

It's your channel, how much you want people to learn from it is really up to you.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: What you describe is why so many women from my generation aren't as involved in trade skills, constantly warned that's "too dangerous for women" while growing up.  That seems to be improving, as I see a lot of women today hosting YouTube channels involving power saws, welding, etc.  But even yesterday I was looking on-line for a small electric trimmer for pruning small branches and ran across a one-handed 4-inch blade "mini chainsaw" where they kept hammering home the point "it's safe enough for a woman!"  Um, excuse me, but why is a regular chainsaw any "less safe" for a woman than a man?  Assuming one chooses a chainsaw that's an appropriate size/weight/balance regardless of their gender, there's no inherently greater risk for a woman than a man in using one.  You don't have to find a ridiculously tiny chainsaw for it to finally be "safe enough for a woman!" author: @judyofthewoods
text:
Spot on! The cuts also get less with time as you learn. 
I am so grateful that my parents encouraged or allowed creativity and adventure. My first solo adventure in creativity and travel were age 5. My father, who worked and lodged a few hundred miles away gave me a box of the wooden equivalent of Mechano. So one day I decided to visit him, made a scooter of said wooden bits, and set off in the snow with the street name and house number in my memory (who needs to know which city, lol). A few hundred yards up the road I came to a junction and had to ask a boy which way to this address. Whether he humoured me or a street of that name also existed in that town, I don't know. In any event he pointed me in some direction. Alas, just as I pushed off a wheel fell off and I had to return home. My mother only told me recently that she followed me at a distance I would not notice her. She never interfered. Things like that gave me the foundation for a life of creating and taking worthwhile risks to the point of enjoying a good dose of OCD - Obsessive Creative Desire.

author: @jimintaos
text: Nicely said.  I am inspired to share my Elwood P. Kyle story.  Something like 40 years ago, when I was a younger man I was working on a construction site with an old guy-Elwood- he was a master carpenter who was the absolute epitome of a Virginia gentleman.  He was watching me use the table saw one day and very politely suggested it would be better if I set the blade no more than 1/8 of an inch (3mm) above the thing I was cutting.  I asked him if the saw didn't cut better when more blade was exposed.  He agreed that it would indeed cut better when more blade was exposed, especially through fingers and thumbs.  It seemed like good advice so I adopted his suggestion and a year or so later when I had an accident on the table saw and sliced through the ball of my thumb it was just a cut in the ball of my thumb and not through the whole of my thumb.  Now that I am an old guy I remember Elwood's lesson.  Not the one about the saw blade height, but the one where you watch someone and when you see the need for a quiet word of advice you step in and offer it.  Hopefully they will learn, if not-----------well that is also a lesson in the making.  All to often young guys watch our mouths moving and hear nothing useful coming out of them.  But, once in a while, like my listening to Elwood and his greater experience, they listen.
